note_data.title: Further organic chemistry
keyPoints: Transition elements are found in the d-block of the periodic table and form one or more stable ions with incomplete d orbitals
Scandium and zinc are in the d-block but are not transition elements because their ions do not have incomplete d subshells
Scandium forms Sc³⁺ with an empty d subshell, zinc forms Zn²⁺ with a full d subshell
The 4s orbital is filled before the 3d orbital, but 4s electrons are removed before 3d electrons when forming ions
Transition elements can form multiple oxidation states (e.g., vanadium forms V²⁺, V³⁺, V⁴⁺ and V⁵⁺)
The d subshell can hold up to 10 electrons
Chromium has the unusual electron configuration [Ar]3d⁵4s¹ and copper has [Ar]3d¹⁰4s¹

definitions.definition: Elements that form one or more stable ions with incomplete d orbitals
A type of orbital that can hold up to 10 electrons and has a specific shape

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
For benzene, always show delocalised structure with circle. For amino acids, show zwitterion form at isoelectric point
Use evidence given to work out structure. Show your working clearly

modelAnswer.question: Explain why phenol is more acidic than ethanol. [3]
modelAnswer.answer: Phenol can lose H⁺ from the -OH group to form a phenoxide ion. The negative charge on the oxygen atom is delocalised into the benzene ring. This delocalisation stabilises the phenoxide ion, making it easier for phenol to lose H⁺ and act as an acid. In ethanol, there is no delocalisation so the ethoxide ion is less stable.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Phenol loses H⁺ to form phenoxide ion
Mark 2: Negative charge is delocalised into benzene ring
Mark 3: Delocalisation stabilises the ion / makes phenol more acidic

commonMistakes: Thinking scandium and zinc are transition elements when they are not due to their complete or empty d subshells in their stable ions
Forgetting that 4s electrons are removed before 3d electrons when forming ions, despite 4s being filled first
Not recognizing the unusual electron configurations of chromium and copper
examinerTips: Remember that transition elements must have incomplete d orbitals in their ions, not just in their atoms
When writing electron configurations for transition metal ions, always remove 4s electrons first
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Benzene delocalised structure showing circle inside hexagon, zwitterion structure of amino acids (H3N⁺-CHR-COO⁻), and peptide bond formation between amino acids with water elimination
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to identify functional groups in complex organic molecules and predict products of elimination reactions
N/D 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice question testing knowledge of addition polymerization mechanisms and monomer identification
M/J 2022 P2 Q5(c) (4 marks) — students had to explain the formation of esters from carboxylic acids and alcohols, including naming the catalyst
N/D 2023 P2 Q3(b) (5 marks) — required drawing structural formulas for addition polymers and explaining the difference between addition and condensation polymerization

workedExample.question: Ethanoic acid reacts with propan-1-ol to form an ester. (a) Name the ester formed (1 mark) (b) Write a balanced equation for this reaction (2 marks) (c) State the catalyst used (1 mark)
steps: Step 1: Identify the reactants - ethanoic acid (CH₃COOH) and propan-1-ol (CH₃CH₂CH₂OH)
Step 2: For ester naming, take 'propyl' from alcohol and 'ethanoate' from acid = propyl ethanoate
Step 3: Write structures showing ester formation: acid + alcohol → ester + water
Step 4: Balance equation: CH₃COOH + CH₃CH₂CH₂OH → CH₃COOCH₂CH₂CH₃ + H₂O
Step 5: Recall that concentrated sulfuric acid catalyzes esterification
workedExample.answer: (a) propyl ethanoate (b) CH₃COOH + CH₃CH₂CH₂OH → CH₃COOCH₂CH₂CH₃ + H₂O (c) concentrated sulfuric acid / H₂SO₄
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct ester name, 1 mark for correct structural formulas, 1 mark for balanced equation, 1 mark for naming catalyst

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused addition and condensation polymerization' — always check if small molecules are eliminated when identifying polymerization type
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently drew incorrect ester structures' — remember the ester link is COO, with the alkyl group from alcohol attached to single oxygen
Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates lost marks by not showing continuation bonds in polymer structures' — always include 'n' subscript and continuation bonds when drawing polymer repeat units
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must draw the formation of addition polymers showing: (1) monomer structure with C=C double bond clearly marked, (2) arrow indicating polymerization conditions, (3) repeat unit in square brackets with continuation bonds extending left and right, (4) subscript 'n' outside brackets, (5) note that double bonds break to form single bonds in polymer chain. Example: nCH₂=CHCl → [-CH₂-CHCl-]ₙ with proper continuation bonds shown.
note_data.quickRecap: Transition elements are d-block elements that form stable ions with incomplete d orbitals. Scandium and zinc are not transition elements despite being in the d-block. When forming ions, 4s electrons are removed before 3d electrons, and many transition elements can form multiple oxidation states.
